text: Maxwell added the displacement current term to Ampere's law. It is:
explanation: Displacement current: \( I_d = \epsilon_0 \frac{d\Phi_E}{dt} \). This resolved the inconsistency in Ampere's law for capacitor circuits and predicted EM waves.
name: Displacement Current

text: The speed of electromagnetic waves in vacuum is:
explanation: Speed of light: \( c = 1/\sqrt{\mu_0\epsilon_0} \approx 3\times10^8\,\text{m/s} \). Maxwell derived this and identified light as EM wave.
name: Speed of EM Waves

text: In an electromagnetic wave, the electric and magnetic fields are:
options: Perpendicular to each other and to direction of propagation
explanation: EM waves: \( \vec{E} \perp \vec{B} \perp \vec{v} \). All three are mutually perpendicular. EM waves are transverse.
name: EM Wave Field Orientation

text: The intensity of an electromagnetic wave is related to field amplitudes by:
explanation: EM wave intensity: \( I = \frac{1}{2}c\epsilon_0 E_0^2 = \frac{c B_0^2}{2\mu_0} \). Also \( E_0/B_0 = c \).
name: EM Wave Intensity

text: The correct order of electromagnetic spectrum from lowest to highest frequency is:
options: Gamma > X-ray > UV > Visible > IR > Microwave > Radio
Radio < Microwave < IR < Visible < UV < X-ray < Gamma
UV < Visible < IR < Microwave < Radio < X-ray < Gamma
X-ray > Visible > Radio > Gamma > IR > Microwave
explanation: EM spectrum (increasing frequency): Radio < Microwave < Infrared < Visible < UV < X-ray < Gamma rays.
name: EM Spectrum Order

text: The relation between E and B amplitudes in an EM wave is:
explanation: \( E_0/B_0 = c \) (speed of light). The electric field amplitude is much larger than magnetic amplitude numerically.
name: E and B Amplitudes in EM Wave

text: Microwaves are used in radar and cooking because:
options: Their wavelength (cm range) allows them to heat food and reflect off objects
explanation: Microwaves (1mm–30cm): wavelength comparable to food molecules. In microwave oven: absorbed by water, causing heating. Radar uses reflection.
name: Uses of Microwaves

options: Rapidly decelerating electrons hitting a metal target (Bremsstrahlung + characteristic X-rays)
explanation: X-rays produced in X-ray tube: electrons accelerated through high voltage hit tungsten target → rapid deceleration → X-ray emission. Also characteristic X-rays from inner shell transitions.
name: Production of X-rays

options: Energy flux (power per unit area) of EM wave: \( \vec{S} = \frac{1}{\mu_0}\vec{E}\times\vec{B} \)
explanation: Poynting vector: \( \vec{S} = \frac{\vec{E}\times\vec{B}}{\mu_0} \). Magnitude = intensity (W/m²). Direction = energy flow direction (same as wave propagation).
name: Poynting Vector

text: The wavelength range of visible light is approximately:
explanation: Visible light: 400 nm (violet) to 700 nm (red). ROYGBIV: Red(700nm)→Orange→Yellow→Green→Blue→Indigo→Violet(400nm).
name: Visible Light Wavelength Range

explanation: IR radiation (heat radiation) detected by: thermopile, bolometer, thermistor, photoconductor. Human skin feels it as warmth. Eyes cannot see IR directly.
name: IR Radiation Detection

explanation: Gamma rays: emitted from nucleus during radioactive decay (α or β decay often followed by γ emission). Highest frequency, highest energy, most penetrating.
name: Gamma Ray Source

text: The radiation pressure of an EM wave of intensity \(I\) on a perfectly absorbing surface is:
explanation: Radiation pressure: \( P = I/c \) (absorbing surface). \( P = 2I/c \) (perfectly reflecting surface). EM waves carry momentum.
name: Radiation Pressure

text: In a medium with refractive index \(n\), the speed of EM wave is:
explanation: Speed in medium: \( v = c/n \), where \( n = \sqrt{\mu_r\epsilon_r} \). Light slows down in denser media (higher \(n\)).
name: Speed of EM Wave in Medium

options: Vitamin D synthesis in skin, sunburn, sterilisation, and photochemical reactions
explanation: UV radiation effects: causes sunburn, skin cancer, but also triggers Vitamin D synthesis. Used in sterilisation (UV kills bacteria) and photolithography.
name: Effects of UV Radiation
